us-politics.headline: Trump's Iran Threats Divide Congress, Spark War Crime Debate
us-politics.body:
President Trump's Easter message threatening Iran has drawn sharp criticism from both parties. Sen. Chris Murphy called the threat a clear war crime, while Rep. Madeleine Dean raised concerns about Trump's proposed budget alongside the Iran standoff. Even Marjorie Taylor Greene broke with the president, calling the rhetoric insane.

The military dimension is already live. Iran says a US aircraft was destroyed during a recovery mission for a missing service member, while Netanyahu confirmed he spoke with Trump and congratulated him on rescuing a downed pilot. The two accounts suggest active and risky US operations in the region.

One House Republican said ground troops will not be needed to open the Strait of Hormuz. Maryland Governor Wes Moore argued ending foreign wars is the fastest route to lower gas prices at home.

sources.title: Chris Murphy on Trump’s Iran threat: ‘A clear war crime’
Iran says US aircraft destroyed during recovery mission for missing service member
Rep. Madeleine Dean discusses Iran and President Trump's proposed budget
House Republican says he doesn’t think ground troops will be needed to open the Strait of Hormuz
Marjorie Taylor Greene rails against ‘insane’ Trump after Easter Iran threat
Moore: Ending foreign wars ‘best thing that we could do’ to lower gas prices

us-politics.headline: Trump Mail-In Voting Order Faces Legal Pushback From Democrats
us-politics.body:
Stacey Abrams called Trump's executive order targeting mail-in voting patently illegal, arguing it oversteps presidential authority and threatens ballot access. The order has reignited a long-running fight over voting rules that split sharply along partisan lines.

Republicans argue mail-in voting invites fraud and that tightening rules protects election integrity. Democrats say the order is a deliberate attempt to suppress turnout among groups that rely heavily on postal ballots. Courts are likely to be the next battleground.

sources.title: Stacey Abrams on Trump’s mail-in voting order: ‘Patently illegal’

us-politics.headline: GOP Splits Stall DHS Reopening as Trump Stays Distant
us-politics.body:
Republican divisions in Congress are blocking efforts to reopen the Department of Homeland Security, with Speaker Johnson and Senate Majority Leader Thune struggling to align their caucuses. Trump's detachment from the negotiations has made the impasse harder to break.

The standoff reflects a broader tension inside the GOP between hardliners who want deeper spending cuts and members wary of the political cost of a prolonged shutdown. No deal appears imminent.

sources.title: Republican divisions, Trump’s detachment stymie GOP efforts to reopen DHS

us-politics.headline: Democrats Defend Cuba Visit, Slam US Fuel Blockade
us-politics.body:
Two Democratic lawmakers returned from Cuba and publicly condemned US policy, describing the restrictions on fuel as an economic bombing of the island. The comments drew immediate criticism from Republicans, who argue the embargo is a necessary response to Cuba's authoritarian government.

The visit highlights a persistent divide over Cuba policy. Democrats who made the trip say ordinary Cubans bear the cost of sanctions. Republicans counter that easing pressure rewards a regime with a long record of human rights abuses.

sources.title: Pair of Democrat lawmakers slam 'blockade of fuel' to Cuba, 'economic bombing' after visit to island

au-politics.headline: Australia's Fuel Crisis Bites Hard Over Easter Long Weekend
au-politics.body:
Australia's ongoing fuel crisis is squeezing households and businesses across the country, with supply shortages pushing prices higher and disrupting Easter travel. An outback pub near Oodnadatta reported just two visitors over the long weekend, with the owner blaming fuel costs for keeping tourists away from remote destinations that depend on holiday traffic to survive.

The government says fuel shipments are secured well into May, but critics argue that is not enough. The Spectator argues Australia should follow the US model of guaranteeing domestic oil supply, pointing to strategic reserve failures as a structural policy problem. Used electric vehicle prices have surged in response, with buyers rushing to insulate themselves from petrol costs. The Guardian's live tracker shows ongoing service station outages and volatile pricing across multiple states.

sources.title: Outback tourist pub had two visitors this Easter amid fuel price pinch
Track current petrol and diesel prices, service station outages and shipments – Australia's fuel crisis in charts
Used EV prices rise as Australia’s fuel crisis hits: ‘Doesn’t make sense to hold on to a combustion engine’
The US guaranteed its oil, why can’t Australia?
Australia's fuel shipments secured 'well into' May

au-politics.headline: Property Market Weakens as Auction Clearance Hits Four-Year Low
au-politics.body:
Australia's national auction clearance rate has fallen to its lowest point since 2022, adding to signs that the property market is cooling under sustained interest rate and cost-of-living pressure. The result marks a notable shift after years of resilience in major city markets.

The data lands during a period of broader economic strain, with fuel costs, housing affordability and household budgets all under pressure simultaneously. The Spectator's commentary on what it calls Australian decadence frames the moment as a broader cultural and economic reckoning, though that view sits well to the right of mainstream economic analysis. The clearance rate drop will likely sharpen debate heading into the federal political cycle about housing policy and the Reserve Bank's next move.

sources.title: Australian decadence
National auction clearance rate hits lowest point since 2022 – as it happened

au-politics.headline: Victoria's Free Train Tickets Backfire as Passengers Left Standing
au-politics.body:
Victoria's free public transport offer over the Easter long weekend overwhelmed regional train services, leaving passengers standing for hours on routes not built for the surge in demand. The policy was designed to ease cost-of-living pressure and reduce road congestion, but the execution drew sharp criticism from stranded travellers.

The incident raises questions about whether the Andrews-era infrastructure investment has kept pace with demand, and whether free-travel incentives need better capacity planning before rollout. It is a rare case where a cost-of-living measure generated its own backlash. The state government has not yet responded publicly to the complaints.

sources.title: Passengers forced to stand for hours on regional Victoria trains as free tickets supercharge long weekend demand

au-politics.headline: Jury Trial Debate and Legal Rights Draw Conservative Warning
au-politics.body:
The Spectator has published a sharp argument against moves to scrap or limit jury trials in Australian courts, framing the issue as a fundamental threat to civil liberties and the common law tradition. The piece warns that efficiency-driven reforms to the justice system risk stripping ordinary citizens of a core democratic safeguard.

The argument sits within a broader conservative critique of institutional change, but the underlying legal debate crosses party lines. Jury trial reform has been floated in various states as courts face backlogs worsened by the pandemic. Supporters of reform argue the system is slow and costly. Critics say speed is no justification for removing a check on state power that has existed for centuries.

sources.title: Scrapping jury trials will cost us all

world.headline: Iran War Escalates as Trump Claims Victory, Hormuz Tensions Rise
world.body:
A US airman shot down over Iran has been rescued in a covert operation, with Trump declaring victory even as his administration issues a deadline to Tehran over the Strait of Hormuz. Iran has flatly rejected the ultimatum. Two people were killed in a strike on Haifa, and BBC reporters near the strait describe conditions at the edge of an active battlefield.

The conflict is rippling far beyond the region. Vietnam's gig workers are absorbing punishing fuel cost increases tied to the war's disruption of global oil markets. A new regional power dynamic around the Strait of Hormuz is already taking shape, with analysts debating who fills the vacuum as the fighting continues.

sources.title: A new regional order for the Strait of Hormuz
Vietnam’s gig workers slammed by rising fuel costs amid fallout of Iran war
Trump declares victory after rescue but threats to US operation in Iran still loom
Iran war live: Tehran rejects Trump’s Hormuz deadline; 2 killed in Haifa
'We are at the edge of a battlefield': BBC reports near Strait of Hormuz
How rescue of US airman in remote part of Iran unfolded

world.headline: Gaza Strikes Continue as Hamas Refuses Disarmament Demands
world.body:
Israel struck a crowd in Gaza City, adding to a mounting civilian toll as the humanitarian situation draws renewed international scrutiny. Hamas's armed wing, the Qassam Brigades, has rejected disarmament as a precondition for any deal, calling the demand unacceptable. Talks appear deadlocked.

Critics argue the humanitarian crisis in Gaza has been systematically sidelined in diplomatic negotiations. Aid access remains severely restricted, and displacement across the region continues to worsen, with Lebanon also reporting communities whose lives have been upended by ongoing conflict spillover.

sources.title: Displaced in Lebanon: 'Lives turned upside down'
Has the humanitarian crisis in Gaza been ignored?
Hamas armed wing says disarmament demands not acceptable
Israeli air strike hits crowd in Gaza City
Hamas armed wing rejects disarmament while Israel continues attacks

world.headline: Nigeria Army Frees 31 Hostages After Church Attack
world.body:
Nigerian army forces rescued 31 hostages following an attack on a church, in one of the more significant counter-insurgency operations the military has carried out in recent months. Details on the perpetrators and the location of the rescue remain limited, but the operation points to continued instability in parts of the country where armed groups target civilian gatherings.

Separately, UK police arrested seven protesters near an RAF base being used by US forces, reflecting growing domestic opposition in Britain to its role in supporting American military operations in the Iran conflict.

sources.title: Nigeria's army rescues 31 hostages after church attack
UK police arrest seven protesters near RAF base used by US

world.headline: Russians in Exile Grieve as Ukraine Deepens Syria Security Ties
world.body:
Russians who fled their country since the war in Ukraine began are navigating grief and displacement with no clear path home, caught between a regime they oppose and countries that view them with suspicion. Their communities abroad are fractured, and many describe mourning losses, both personal and national, in isolation.

Ukraine is meanwhile expanding its strategic footprint, with President Zelenskyy announcing a security and military cooperation agreement with Syria. The deal signals Kyiv's effort to build alliances beyond its immediate neighborhood as the war with Russia grinds on.

sources.title: Russians living in exile cope with grief far from home
Ukraine and Syria to cooperate on security, Zelenskyy says

ai.headline: Gemma 4 Goes Local: Google's Model Runs Everywhere
ai.body:
Google's Gemma 4 is having a moment. Developers are running it on iPhones via the Google AI Edge Gallery app, spinning it up locally with LM Studio's headless CLI, and building real-time audio/video pipelines on M3 Pro MacBooks. The model is landing across hardware that would have seemed implausible for on-device AI just a year ago.

Separately, a browser-embedded project called Gemma Gem lets users run the model with no API keys and no cloud dependency at all. The pattern is clear: smaller, capable open models are fragmenting away from centralized inference. Developers are not waiting for hosted APIs when the hardware in their pocket or on their desk can handle the job.

sources.title: Show HN: Gemma Gem – AI model embedded in a browser – no API keys, no cloud
Gemma 4 on iPhone
Show HN: Real-time AI (audio/video in, voice out) on an M3 Pro with Gemma E2B
Running Gemma 4 locally with LM Studio's new headless CLI and Claude Code

ai.headline: Microsoft Calls Copilot 'Entertainment Only' in Fine Print
ai.body:
Microsoft's terms of service quietly classify Copilot as being for entertainment purposes only, a designation that strips users of any reasonable expectation of reliability or accuracy. For a product Microsoft markets aggressively to enterprise customers, the legal hedge is striking.

The disclosure lands alongside a broader critique that Microsoft has lacked a coherent software strategy for decades. Critics argue the company layers new products onto shaky foundations without resolving the underlying architectural confusion. Copilot's disclaimer reads less like legal caution and more like an admission that the product is not ready to be trusted with real work.

sources.title: Copilot is ‘for entertainment purposes only,’ according to Microsoft’s terms of use
Microsoft hasn't had a coherent GUI strategy since Petzold

ai.headline: Japan's Labor Shortage Is Proving Physical AI Works
ai.body:
Japan is deploying robots not to displace workers but to fill roles that go unfilled. An aging population and a cultural resistance to mass immigration have created acute shortages in logistics, elder care, and hospitality. Physical AI systems are stepping into that gap, and the results are showing that real-world deployment is more viable than lab demos suggested.

The country is functioning as an accidental proving ground. Robots operating in messy, unpredictable environments are accumulating the kind of edge-case experience that simulation cannot replicate. What Japan is learning now will shape how the rest of the world thinks about physical AI deployment.

sources.title: In Japan, the robot isn't coming for your job; it's filling the one nobody wants
In Japan, the robot isn’t coming for your job; it’s filling the one nobody wants

ai.headline: Employers Mine Personal Data to Lowball Salary Offers
ai.body:
Companies are using personal data, drawn from social profiles, spending patterns, and employment history, to estimate the minimum salary a candidate will accept before making an offer. The practice turns the negotiation table sharply in the employer's favor, letting them anchor offers below what they might otherwise pay.

The tactic is a direct consequence of the data economy reaching into hiring. Workers rarely know what signals they are broadcasting or how those signals are being scored. The asymmetry is significant: employers arrive at negotiations with a data-derived floor, while candidates are still guessing.

sources.title: Employers use your personal data to figure out the lowest salary you'll accept

btc.headline: Bitcoin Bounces to $69K as Geopolitical Tensions Ease
btc.body:
Bitcoin reclaimed $69,000 Monday as ceasefire talks and a delayed Trump deadline on Iran lifted sentiment across crypto markets. Short sellers got caught offside, amplifying the move higher as forced buybacks added fuel. XRP edged up to $1.33 but remains stuck in a tight range, suggesting broader conviction is still thin.

Not everyone is buying the recovery. One analyst warns Bitcoin faces a collapse to $10,000 unless prices can reclaim $75,000 and hold it. Polymarket puts the odds of a US invasion of Iran this year at 63%, meaning the geopolitical risk that briefly lifted markets could just as quickly reverse them.

sources.title: Bitcoin reclaims $69,000 as ceasefire talks surface and crypto shorts get squeezed
Bitcoin meltdown to $10,000 remains likely unless prices reclaim $75,000, analyst says
Trump-Iran deadline chaos sends crypto higher while ceasefire hopes rise
XRP drifts higher to $1.33, but range-bound trade still dominates
Polymarket odds of US invading Iran this year reach 63% after Trump's post

btc.headline: Circle Builds Quantum Defenses Into Its New Layer-1 Blockchain
btc.body:
Circle has unveiled a quantum-resistant roadmap for Arc, its forthcoming layer-1 blockchain, baking post-quantum cryptography in from the start rather than retrofitting it later. The move positions Arc ahead of most existing chains, which remain vulnerable to the threat quantum computers pose to standard elliptic-curve encryption.

The timing is deliberate. Quantum hardware is advancing faster than most blockchain developers have planned for, and Circle is betting that security-first infrastructure will matter to institutional users of its stablecoin products. A broader explainer published this week underscores the stakes - a sufficiently powerful quantum computer could theoretically crack Bitcoin's private keys.

sources.title: Circle future-proofs Arc blockchain against quantum threats
Circle unveils quantum-resistant roadmap for its layer-1 blockchain Arc
A simple explainer on what quantum computing actually is, and why it is terrifying for bitcoin

btc.headline: North Korea Has Quietly Worked Inside DeFi for Seven Years
btc.body:
North Korean IT workers have been embedding themselves inside DeFi protocols since the 2020 DeFi summer, according to a cybersecurity researcher - far longer than most in the industry have acknowledged. The operatives worked on real codebases, earning salaries and siphoning intelligence, while their identities went undetected across countless projects.

The revelation reframes past exploits. Many high-profile DeFi hacks may have had inside knowledge behind them rather than purely external attacks. The findings add pressure on protocols to tighten contributor vetting, a process the decentralized nature of these projects makes genuinely difficult.

sources.title: North Korean workers have been infiltrating DeFi for 7 years: Researcher

btc.headline: Saylor Eyes More Bitcoin; Bitchat Pulled From China App Store
btc.body:
Michael Saylor signaled Strategy is preparing another Bitcoin purchase after a week-long pause, keeping markets alert to a buy announcement that has historically nudged prices upward. Meanwhile, Apple removed Jack Dorsey's Bitchat app from its China App Store at Beijing's request. Bitchat uses Bitcoin's Lightning Network for messaging, making it a censorship-resistance tool that Chinese authorities clearly found threatening.

Rwanda also moved to block Bybit's peer-to-peer platform, which had been offering franc-to-crypto trading, citing regulatory concerns. Separately, a crypto attorney flagged that the recent Drift protocol incident could expose its operators to civil negligence claims - another sign that legal accountability in DeFi is tightening.

sources.title: Michael Saylor hints at next Strategy Bitcoin buy after weeklong pause
Apple pulls Jack Dorsey's Bitchat from China at Beijing’s request
Rwanda swats Bybit’s P2P platform offering franc-to-crypto trading
Crypto attorney says Drift incident may qualify as 'civil negligence'

food.name: Miso-Glazed Aubergine (Nasu Dengaku)
food.description: Halved aubergines broiled until silky and caramelised under a sweet-savoury miso and mirin glaze
food.tip: Score the flesh in a crosshatch before glazing so the miso penetrates deeper and the surface blisters beautifully rather than burning

exercise.name: Monday Reset: Loaded Carry Circuit
exercise.description: Alternate between farmer carries, suitcase carries, and overhead carries across a set distance, building grip strength, core stability, and posture after a weekend of likely sitting

learn.topic: The Ship of Theseus and Personal Identity Over Time
learn.description: The ancient paradox asks whether an object that has had all its components replaced remains fundamentally the same object — philosophers have extended this to human cells, memory, and selfhood
learn.why: On a Monday at the start of a new week it is a quietly unsettling and productive question to sit with: the person who made last week's decisions has been partially replaced, and understanding why that feels wrong reveals a great deal about how identity is constructed rather than discovered
